sets.passage:
Sourdough bread occupies an unusual place in the modern kitchen. Unlike loaves raised with commercial baker's yeast, a sourdough loaf depends on a living culture that the baker cultivates, feeds and passes on, sometimes for many years. This culture, often called a starter, is a stable community of wild yeasts and bacteria that thrive together in a mixture of flour and water. The chemistry of what happens inside that modest paste is far more intricate than most people assume, and it explains why sourdough tastes, smells and behaves so differently from ordinary bread.

At the heart of the process lies fermentation, the conversion of sugars into other compounds by microorganisms deprived of oxygen. When flour and water are combined and left at room temperature, microbes already present on the grain and in the surrounding air begin to feed on the starches and simple sugars available. Two broad groups dominate. Wild yeasts, chiefly species of the genus Saccharomyces and its relatives, consume sugars and release carbon dioxide, the gas that inflates the dough and gives bread its open, airy structure. Alongside them work lactic acid bacteria, which produce the acids responsible for sourdough's characteristic tang. These two populations are not rivals so much as partners; the acids generated by the bacteria create conditions in which the particular yeasts of the culture flourish, while the yeasts release nutrients that the bacteria can use in turn.

The balance between these organisms is delicate and responsive. Temperature, the type of flour, the ratio of water to flour, and the frequency of feeding all shift the community in subtle ways. A starter kept warm tends to favour bacteria that yield a sharper, more acidic flavour, whereas a cooler starter often produces a milder loaf. Bakers who understand these variables can steer the outcome, coaxing a culture toward the taste they prefer. This is why two bakers using apparently identical recipes may produce breads that differ noticeably in aroma and crumb.

One of the most striking claims associated with sourdough is that it may be gentler on digestion than bread made with commercial yeast. The extended fermentation typical of sourdough gives microbes time to break down some of the components of flour that certain people find difficult to process, including particular sugars and portions of the gluten proteins. It would be misleading, however, to describe sourdough as suitable for those with a genuine medical intolerance to gluten, since meaningful amounts of the protein remain. What the evidence more cautiously suggests is that the slow transformation of the dough alters its composition in ways that some digestive systems tolerate more comfortably.

The long history of sourdough reinforces how fundamental the technique is. Before isolated, standardised yeast became commercially available in the nineteenth century, virtually all leavened bread relied on wild cultures of one kind or another. Communities maintained their starters carefully, and the flavour of a region's bread was, in a real sense, a product of its local microbes. The rise of industrial yeast made baking faster and more predictable, and for much of the twentieth century sourdough retreated to the margins. Its recent revival owes something to a broader interest in traditional foods and in the microbial life that shapes them.

Scientists studying starters have found that these cultures are remarkably resilient. A well-maintained starter resists invasion by unwanted microbes, partly because the acids it produces make the environment inhospitable to many spoilage organisms. This self-protecting quality helps explain how a starter can survive for decades with nothing more than regular feeding. It also illustrates a wider principle in microbiology: stable communities of cooperating organisms can be far more robust than any single species alone.

For the baker, all of this translates into a craft that rewards patience and attention. A loaf cannot be rushed, because the flavour and texture depend on giving the culture enough time to do its work. Yet the reward is a bread with genuine depth of character, one that reflects the particular history of its starter and the choices of the person tending it. In this sense a sourdough loaf is less a manufactured product than the visible result of an ongoing partnership between a human being and an invisible community of living things.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?
options: Sourdough is healthier than all other types of bread and should replace them.
Sourdough bread results from a complex, cultivated microbial partnership that shapes its distinctive qualities.
Commercial yeast has made traditional baking methods completely obsolete.
The flavour of bread depends entirely on the skill of the individual baker.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what role do lactic acid bacteria play in a starter?
options: They produce the carbon dioxide that makes the dough rise.
They compete with yeasts and eventually eliminate them.
They generate acids that give sourdough its tang and help the yeasts flourish.
They remove all gluten from the flour during fermentation.

questions.prompt: What does the passage state about the effect of temperature on a starter?
options: A warmer starter tends to favour bacteria that yield a sharper, more acidic flavour.
Temperature has no measurable effect on the taste of the finished bread.
A cooler starter always produces bread that fails to rise.
Only professional bakers can detect any difference caused by temperature.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the distinctive flavour of a region's traditional bread was largely due to
options: the superior wheat grown only in certain regions.
secret recipes guarded by individual families.
the use of standardised commercial yeast.
the local wild microbes that populated its starters.

questions.prompt: In the seventh paragraph, the word "inhospitable" is closest in meaning to
options: unwelcoming or unsuitable for survival.
warm and comfortable.
rich in available nutrients.
openly friendly.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's main purpose in the final paragraph?
options: To warn readers that home baking is too difficult for most people.
To argue that machines produce better bread than human bakers.
To portray sourdough baking as a rewarding partnership requiring patience and care.
To provide a step-by-step recipe for making a loaf.

sets.explain_zh: 本文说明酸面包依赖酵母菌与乳酸菌共生发酵，风味由培养物与烘焙者共同塑造。题目考查主旨、细节、推断、词义与作者态度。

sets.passage:
Along many temperate coastlines, oyster reefs were once among the most productive features of the marine landscape. Beds of these shellfish, sometimes rising several metres from the seabed, formed sprawling structures that supported an extraordinary variety of life. Over the past two centuries, however, the great majority of the world's native oyster reefs have disappeared, victims of overharvesting, disease and the degradation of the waters in which they lived. In response, marine scientists and conservation groups have begun ambitious efforts to bring these reefs back, and their work is reshaping how people think about the value of coastal ecosystems.

The appeal of oyster reefs extends far beyond the shellfish themselves. A single adult oyster can filter many litres of water each day, drawing in tiny particles and suspended matter as it feeds. Across a whole reef, this filtering capacity is enormous, and it can measurably improve the clarity of the surrounding water. Clearer water allows more sunlight to reach the seabed, which in turn helps underwater plants such as seagrasses to grow. These plants provide shelter and food for young fish and other creatures, so the benefits of a healthy reef ripple outward through the wider ecosystem. In this way an oyster reef acts less like a single organism and more like a piece of living infrastructure.

Reefs also offer a form of natural coastal defence. Their hard, uneven surfaces break the force of incoming waves, reducing the energy that reaches the shore. Along low-lying coasts vulnerable to erosion and storm surges, this buffering effect can protect marshes, mudflats and even human settlements. As the costs of building and maintaining concrete sea walls have risen, and as the limitations of such structures have become clearer, planners have grown increasingly interested in restored reefs as a cheaper and more flexible alternative that can, unlike a wall, repair and extend itself over time.

Restoring a reef is not simply a matter of returning oysters to the water. Young oysters, known as spat, need a firm surface on which to settle and grow, and in many degraded areas the old shell material that once provided this foundation has been lost. Restoration projects therefore often begin by laying down a base of recycled shell, stone or other hard material to which larvae can attach. Some programmes collect used shells from restaurants for precisely this purpose, giving waste material a second life on the seabed. Once a stable foundation exists, oysters can begin to accumulate, and over several years a functioning reef may take shape.

Success, however, is far from guaranteed. Restored reefs remain vulnerable to the same pressures that destroyed the originals. Poor water quality, disease and unregulated harvesting can undo years of effort. For this reason, the most durable projects tend to combine the physical work of rebuilding with broader measures, such as improving the management of the surrounding fishery and reducing the flow of pollutants from the land. Scientists emphasise that a reef is embedded in its environment, and that restoring the structure without addressing the conditions around it is unlikely to produce lasting results.

Monitoring plays a central part in modern restoration. Researchers track the density of oysters, the rate at which young settle, and the return of other species to judge whether a project is working. Such measurements have revealed that restored reefs can attract a surprising range of animals within a relatively short period, suggesting that these ecosystems retain a capacity to recover when given the right start. At the same time, the data serve as a reminder that recovery is gradual and that patience is essential.

Perhaps the most significant shift brought about by this work is one of perception. For a long time oysters were valued chiefly as food, and reefs were regarded as resources to be exploited. The science of restoration has helped to reveal the many other services these reefs provide, from cleaning water to protecting shorelines and sheltering wildlife. Recognising this fuller picture changes the calculation of what a reef is worth. When the many benefits are taken into account, investing in the recovery of oyster reefs begins to look not like an act of charity toward nature but a practical and far-sighted use of resources.

questions.prompt: What is the central argument of the passage?
options: Oyster reefs are valuable mainly as a source of seafood for coastal communities.
Restoring oyster reefs delivers wide-ranging ecological and practical benefits that justify the effort.
Concrete sea walls are always superior to natural forms of coastal defence.
Oyster reefs cannot be restored once they have been lost.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how does the filtering activity of oysters benefit seagrasses?
options: It removes competing plants from the seabed.
It supplies the seagrasses directly with food particles.
It clears the water so that more sunlight can reach the seabed.
It raises the temperature of the surrounding water.

questions.prompt: Why do restoration projects often lay down recycled shell or stone?
options: To provide a firm surface on which young oysters can settle.
To prevent fish from entering the reef area.
To replace seagrasses that have been lost.
To mark the boundaries of protected fishing zones.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that a restoration project focused only on rebuilding reef structure would probably
options: succeed more quickly than projects with broader aims.
attract no marine life at all.
be cheaper than maintaining a concrete sea wall in every case.
fail to last if the surrounding water quality and harvesting were not addressed.

questions.prompt: In the second paragraph, the phrase "living infrastructure" is used to suggest that a reef
options: is a fragile organism easily destroyed by waves.
functions like a built structure that supports and benefits a wider system.
must be constructed entirely from artificial materials.
provides no benefit beyond producing oysters.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward oyster reef restoration in the final paragraph?
options: Sceptical that it can ever be worthwhile.
Indifferent to its outcomes.
Supportive, presenting it as a practical and far-sighted investment.
Concerned that it distracts from more important conservation work.

sets.explain_zh: 本文论述牡蛎礁修复带来的净水、护岸与栖息地等多重效益，并强调需同时改善周边环境。题目涵盖主旨、细节、推断、词义与作者态度。

sets.passage:
For most of its history, archaeology was a discipline of the ground. To learn what lay beneath the surface, researchers had to dig, and digging is slow, costly and destructive. A site can be excavated only once, and every trench removes evidence that can never be replaced. In recent decades, however, a quiet transformation has taken place. By looking down from space, archaeologists have found a way to survey vast landscapes without disturbing a single grain of soil, and in doing so they have rewritten parts of the human story.

The key lies in the kinds of images that satellites can now capture. Ordinary photographs record only the light that human eyes can see, but many satellites gather information across a much wider range of the electromagnetic spectrum, including wavelengths beyond human vision. Buried structures, though invisible on the surface, often leave faint traces in the vegetation and soil above them. A buried stone wall, for example, may hold less moisture than the earth around it, so plants growing directly over it may be slightly smaller or a different shade of green. Such differences are usually too subtle to notice from the ground, but from orbit, and especially in images that record infrared light, they can stand out clearly.

One of the most celebrated applications of this technology has been in regions where ancient settlements lie hidden beneath deserts or dense vegetation. Researchers analysing satellite imagery have identified the outlines of long-forgotten towns, road networks and field systems, sometimes across areas far too large to survey on foot in a reasonable time. These discoveries do not replace excavation; rather, they guide it. Knowing in advance where promising features lie allows archaeologists to plan their digs with precision, concentrating their limited resources on the most rewarding locations and avoiding fruitless effort elsewhere.

Satellite archaeology has also proved valuable in protecting sites that are already known. In many parts of the world, ancient remains are threatened by looting, urban expansion and agriculture. By comparing images of the same location taken at different times, researchers can detect the tell-tale scars of illegal digging or the encroachment of new construction. This kind of monitoring offers a way to keep watch over remote and dangerous regions that would be impossible or unsafe to visit regularly. Evidence gathered from space has been used to document the destruction of heritage and to press for its protection.

The approach is not without limitations. Satellite images reveal patterns, but they cannot by themselves confirm what those patterns mean. A promising shape in the vegetation might indicate a buried structure, or it might result from natural features, modern activity or simple coincidence. Interpretation therefore demands caution and expertise, and the findings almost always require confirmation on the ground. Overreliance on imagery, without the checking that fieldwork provides, can lead researchers astray. The most successful projects treat satellite data as a powerful first step rather than a final answer.

The cost and accessibility of the technology have changed dramatically over time. Early satellite imagery was expensive and available only to governments and well-funded institutions. As more satellites have been launched and as older archives have been opened to the public, images have become far easier and cheaper to obtain. This democratisation has allowed researchers with modest budgets, and even interested members of the public, to take part in the search for hidden sites. Some projects now invite volunteers to scan images and flag features of possible interest, harnessing many pairs of eyes to cover ground that a small team never could.

What emerges from all of this is a discipline that has expanded its field of view, quite literally, from the trench to the region and beyond. The ability to see the broad patterns of past human activity, spread across whole landscapes, has revealed connections that ground-level work alone could never have shown. Ancient roads can be traced across hundreds of kilometres; the relationship between a city and its surrounding farmland can be mapped; and the scale of past civilisations can be appreciated in a way that was previously impossible. Archaeology remains, in the end, a science of physical evidence recovered from the earth. But the view from above has given it a wholly new sense of scale.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the main point of the passage?
options: Satellite imagery has made excavation completely unnecessary.
Ancient roads can only be discovered through traditional digging.
Satellite images are too unreliable to be of any use to archaeologists.
Satellite technology has transformed archaeology by allowing researchers to survey and protect sites on a vast scale.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why might plants growing over a buried stone wall look different?
options: The wall releases chemicals that colour the plants.
The soil over the wall may hold less moisture, affecting plant growth.
Archaeologists deliberately mark such plants for study.
Satellites project light that changes the plants' appearance.

questions.prompt: How, according to the passage, is satellite imagery used to protect known sites?
options: By physically preventing looters from reaching remote areas.
By replacing the need for any excavation at those sites.
By comparing images taken at different times to detect looting or construction.
By training local guards to patrol every location.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that a researcher who relied on satellite images without any fieldwork would risk
options: misinterpreting natural or modern features as ancient structures.
discovering too many genuine sites to investigate.
being unable to obtain any images at all.
confirming every finding beyond doubt.

questions.prompt: In the sixth paragraph, the word "democratisation" refers to the fact that the technology has become
options: more widely accessible to people beyond wealthy institutions.
controlled entirely by elected governments.
less accurate than it used to be.
restricted to a small group of experts.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's main purpose in the final paragraph?
options: To argue that ground-level archaeology should be abandoned.
To emphasise how the technology has broadened archaeology's scale while remaining rooted in physical evidence.
To criticise archaeologists for resisting new methods.
To predict that satellites will soon replace all archaeologists.

sets.explain_zh: 本文讲述卫星影像如何通过多光谱观测帮助考古学家发现并保护遗址，同时强调仍需实地验证。题目考查主旨、细节、推断、词义与作者写作目的。

sets.passage:
The desire to capture a lasting image of the visible world is very old, but for most of human history it could be satisfied only by the hand of an artist. The invention of photography in the early nineteenth century changed this profoundly, offering for the first time a way to fix an image formed by light itself, without the intervention of a painter's brush. The consequences of this innovation reached far beyond the studios of the first photographers and reshaped the way people recorded, remembered and understood the world around them.

The underlying principle had been known for centuries. Long before photography, artists and scientists were familiar with the camera obscura, a darkened room or box in which light passing through a small hole projected an inverted image of the scene outside onto an opposite surface. The image was vivid but fleeting; the moment the light changed, it was gone. The great challenge, then, was not to form an image but to make it permanent. This required finding materials that would react to light and, crucially, a means of halting that reaction so that the picture would not simply darken into nothing on further exposure.

The breakthroughs came in the 1830s, when several inventors, working largely independently, arrived at practical methods almost simultaneously. Their processes differed considerably. One produced a single, exquisitely detailed image on a polished metal plate, unrepeatable and requiring a long exposure. Another created a picture on paper from which multiple copies could be made, though the results were softer and less sharp. This distinction, between a unique object and a reproducible one, would prove enormously important, for it was the second approach that eventually allowed photographs to be printed and distributed in large numbers.

Early photography was demanding work. Exposures could last many minutes, which made portraits an ordeal; sitters had to remain perfectly still, sometimes with their heads held in place by hidden supports, and the strain often shows in the solemn expressions of the earliest portraits. The chemistry was cumbersome and occasionally hazardous, and photographers frequently had to prepare and develop their plates on the spot. Despite these difficulties, the demand for photographs grew with remarkable speed. Portrait studios opened in towns and cities, and for the first time ordinary people, not merely the wealthy who could afford painted portraits, could own an accurate likeness of themselves and their families.

The uses of the new medium multiplied quickly. Photographers travelled to distant lands and brought back images of landscapes, monuments and peoples that most of their audience would never see in person. These pictures fed a growing public appetite for knowledge of the wider world, though they also carried the particular viewpoints and assumptions of those who took them. Photography entered the service of science as well, recording specimens and experiments with a precision that written description could not match. It was even turned upon events of great consequence, and the earliest attempts to photograph conflict brought home to distant viewers something of the reality of war, unsettling in a way that heroic paintings had never been.

Part of what made photography so powerful was the widespread belief that it told the truth. A photograph seemed to be a direct trace of reality, a record made by light rather than shaped by human hands, and therefore free of the interpretation inherent in painting. This assumption was never entirely justified. Photographers chose what to include and exclude, how to arrange their subjects and when to release the shutter, and even the earliest practitioners sometimes altered their images. Yet the sense that a photograph offered objective evidence gave the medium an authority that other forms of picture-making lacked, and that authority shaped its role in journalism, science and law for generations.

Seen in retrospect, the invention of photography marks a decisive turn in the history of images. It broke the long monopoly of the trained artist over the making of pictures and placed a new kind of visual record within reach of ordinary people. In doing so it altered not only how the world was depicted but how it was remembered, for the photograph offered a means of holding on to a particular moment with a fidelity that memory alone could never provide.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Photography was a minor development that had little effect beyond art studios.
The invention of photography transformed how people recorded and understood the world.
Painted portraits remained more popular than photographs throughout the nineteenth century.
The camera obscura was more important than photography itself.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what was the great challenge in developing photography?
options: Forming an image inside a darkened box.
Finding artists willing to abandon painting.
Persuading the public that images had value.
Making the light-formed image permanent and stopping it from darkening further.

questions.prompt: Why did early portrait sitters often appear solemn, according to the passage?
options: Photographers instructed them to look serious.
The chemicals used gave their faces a stern appearance.
Long exposures forced them to remain perfectly still.
It was considered fashionable to avoid smiling.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that the process producing images on paper was historically significant because
options: it allowed photographs to be copied and distributed widely.
it created sharper images than the metal-plate method.
it required no chemistry at all.
it was cheaper to view but impossible to reproduce.

questions.prompt: In the sixth paragraph, the word "trace" is closest in meaning to
options: a small remaining amount of a substance.
a direct physical record or imprint of something real.
a rough drawing made by hand.
a route followed across a landscape.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward the belief that photographs always tell the truth?
options: The writer fully endorses it as entirely justified.
The writer dismisses photography as pure deception.
The writer considers the question unimportant.
The writer regards it as influential but not wholly accurate.

sets.explain_zh: 本文回顾摄影术的发明、早期困难与用途，并讨论照片“真实性”的观念。题目考查主旨、细节、推断、词义与作者态度。

sets.passage:
When people think of pollution, they usually picture dirty air, contaminated water or discarded waste. Sound rarely comes to mind. Yet noise is now recognised as a genuine pollutant, one that spreads through both land and water and that can disturb, harm and even displace the animals exposed to it. As human activity has grown louder over the past century, the natural soundscapes in which wildlife evolved have been increasingly drowned out, with consequences that researchers are only beginning to measure.

For many animals, sound is not a background feature of the environment but a vital channel of information. Birds sing to defend territory and attract mates; frogs call across ponds; predators listen for the faint movements of prey while prey listen for the approach of danger. In the ocean, where light penetrates only a short distance, sound travels far and fast, and many marine species depend on it almost entirely. Whales and dolphins, for instance, use sound both to communicate across great distances and to build a picture of their surroundings. When human noise floods these channels, it can mask the signals that animals rely on, in much the same way that a loud crowd makes conversation difficult.

The sources of this noise are varied. On land, roads, aircraft and industry generate a more or less constant hum that carries far beyond its origin. In the sea, shipping is a major contributor, and the low rumble of engines can spread over enormous distances underwater. Other activities, such as construction and certain methods of searching for resources beneath the seabed, produce sudden, intense bursts of sound that can be especially damaging. Together these sources have raised the general level of background noise well above what existed in earlier times.

The effects on wildlife take several forms. The most obvious is interference with communication. A bird that cannot be heard over traffic may struggle to attract a mate or warn of danger, and studies have found that some birds living in noisy areas sing at a higher pitch or at different times of day in an apparent attempt to be heard. Such adjustments may help, but they may also carry hidden costs, and not every species is able to adapt. Beyond communication, noise can cause animals to abandon otherwise suitable habitat, avoiding areas that are simply too loud. In this way a stretch of forest or coastline may lose its wildlife not because the food or shelter has gone, but because the noise has driven the animals away.

Noise can also affect animals in subtler physiological ways. Persistent loud sound has been linked to signs of stress, and stress in turn can influence feeding, breeding and the ability to resist disease. In extreme cases, particularly in the ocean, intense noise can cause direct physical harm to sensitive hearing organs. Because so many of these effects are invisible and gradual, they are easily overlooked, which is one reason noise pollution has received far less attention than more visible forms of environmental damage.

There is, however, an encouraging feature that distinguishes noise from many other pollutants. Unlike a chemical spill or plastic waste, noise leaves no lasting residue. The moment the source is switched off, the pollution ends. This means that measures to reduce noise can bring almost immediate relief to affected wildlife. Quieter ship designs, restrictions on when and where the loudest activities may take place, and the careful routing of traffic away from sensitive areas can all lower the burden of sound. During periods when human activity has slowed, researchers have observed that natural soundscapes can return with surprising speed, and that some animals respond quickly to the renewed quiet.

Recognising noise as a form of pollution reframes it as a problem that can be managed rather than an unavoidable by-product of modern life. The tools already exist to make the human world quieter, and the potential benefits extend to people as well, since excessive noise affects human health too. What has been lacking is not the means but the attention. As awareness grows, there is reason to hope that the value of quiet, both for wildlife and for ourselves, will come to be more widely appreciated, and that the natural soundscapes still remaining will be given the protection they deserve.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Noise is a harmless part of the natural environment.
Noise is a real form of pollution that harms wildlife but can be managed and reduced.
Marine animals are the only creatures affected by noise.
Reducing noise is impossible with current technology.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why is sound especially important to many marine species?
options: Because the ocean is completely silent without them.
Because light travels only a short distance underwater, so animals rely on sound to communicate and sense surroundings.
Because sound helps them float more easily.
Because they cannot detect movement in any other way.

questions.prompt: What adaptation have some birds in noisy areas been found to make?
options: They stop singing altogether.
They build nests closer to roads.
They sing at a higher pitch or at different times of day.
They migrate to entirely different continents.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred from the passage that a quiet forest losing its wildlife to noise differs from other habitat loss because
options: the food and shelter may remain intact even though the animals leave.
the noise permanently poisons the soil and water.
the animals are physically unable to move away.
no animals ever lived there in the first place.

questions.prompt: In the sixth paragraph, the word "residue" is closest in meaning to
options: a lingering physical remainder left behind.
a sudden loud noise.
a protective barrier.
a form of energy.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude in the final paragraph?
options: Pessimistic that anything can be done about noise.
Hopeful that greater awareness will lead to better protection of natural soundscapes.
Indifferent to the effects of noise on wildlife.
Convinced that noise benefits both people and animals.

sets.explain_zh: 本文论述噪声污染对野生动物在通讯、栖息与生理上的危害，并指出噪声一旦停止即消失、可被管理。题目考查主旨、细节、推断、词义与作者态度。
